impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act when the unpatented products have substantial non-infringing uses.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the jury's finding that Ingevity engaged in unlawful tying by conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the antitrust liability and corresponding damages award, finding the jury could determine that the unpatented honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's patent-law defense.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of dominant patent holders benefit from this decision, which reinforces that patent rights cannot be leveraged to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who bundle licensing with product purchase requirements face significant antitrust exposure, particularly where the tied products have uses outside the patent's claims. Companies with similar licensing practices should reassess tying arrangements.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on inequitable conduct in patent prosecution and on a Walker Process fraud claim for attempted monopolization based on a fraudulently obtained patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud claims related to Tenaris's coiled tubing patents. The court found genuine disputes of material fact existed regarding whether Tenaris committed inequitable conduct by failing to disclose prior CYMAX technology documents during patent prosecution, and whether Global Tubing could establish Walker Process fraud for attempted monopolization. The case was remanded for trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from the court's willingness to send both inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims to trial, keeping the door open for antitrust remedies against patents obtained through fraud.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased litigation risk when they acquire prior art through asset purchases and subsequently obtain patents in the same technology space without disclosing that prior art during prosecution.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patents claiming server-side tailoring of media content specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-eligible under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court and held that GoTV's patents claiming systems for tailoring media content to wireless device capabilities are ineligible for patenting under § 101. Despite a jury verdict finding Netflix infringed the '715 patent and awarding $2.5 million in damages, the court entered judgment for Netflix. The court also reversed an indefiniteness ruling and vacated rulings on induced infringement and damages evidence.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies like Netflix and other streaming services gain protection from patent infringement claims based on abstract concepts of content adaptation, reinforcing § 101 as a defense against broad software method patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the media streaming and content delivery space face heightened risk that patents directed to server-side content tailoring will be invalidated under § 101, potentially wiping out significant litigation investments and damages awards.

impacts.caseName: US Patent No. 7,679,637 LLC v. Google LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims directed to a web conferencing system with time-shifting capabilities for asynchronous viewing of data streams state patent-eligible subject matter sufficient to survive a motion to dismiss.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's grant of Google's motion to dismiss the patent infringement complaint for failure to state a claim. The '637 patent related to web conferencing systems enabling participants to observe sessions in real-time, delayed, or after completion at different playback rates. The court found the claims failed to state a claim upon which relief can be granted.
impacts.positiveImplications: Major technology companies benefit from the court's willingness to resolve patent eligibility at the motion-to-dismiss stage, reducing litigation costs for defending against weak patent assertions.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face increased difficulty in surviving early-stage motions to dismiss, particularly for software patents directed to conventional computing functionality like media playback and conferencing.

impacts.caseName: Seagen Inc. v. Daiichi Sankyo Company, Ltd.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether claims to antibody-drug conjugates with tetrapeptide linker units satisfy the written description and enablement requirements of 35 U.S.C. § 112 when the specification discloses only dipeptide linkers.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a $41+ million jury verdict in Seagen's favor, holding that the asserted claims of the '039 patent directed to antibody-drug conjugates with tetrapeptide linker units were invalid for lack of written description and enablement. The court found that the specification's disclosure of dipeptide linkers was insufficient to support the broader genus of tetrapeptide claims, despite the jury having found willful infringement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Pharmaceutical and biotech companies accused of patent infringement benefit from the court's rigorous application of written description and enablement requirements, particularly for genus claims that extend beyond specifically disclosed embodiments.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the life sciences sector face heightened invalidity risk for claims that extend to broader structural genera than specifically exemplified in the specification, potentially undermining broad patent portfolios in the ADC space.

impacts.caseName: Coda Development s.r.o. v. Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly granted judgment as a matter of law overturning a $64 million jury verdict for trade secret misappropriation and denying correction of inventorship of a self-inflating tire patent.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's JMOL overturning a $64 million jury verdict ($2.8M compensatory plus $61.2M punitive) finding Goodyear misappropriated Coda's trade secrets relating to self-inflating tire technology. The court also affirmed denial of correction of inventorship for Goodyear's '586 patent. The court found insufficient evidence to support the jury's misappropriation findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Large manufacturers benefit from the court's willingness to overturn substantial jury verdicts in trade secret cases where evidence of misappropriation is insufficient, providing protection against speculative claims by technology collaborators.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology developers and smaller companies that share proprietary information with larger corporate partners face difficulty sustaining trade secret misappropriation verdicts even after achieving favorable jury outcomes.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution constitute a 'general statement of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's ruling that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under APA § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution, the Director retains ultimate authority over institution decisions, and the instructions do not purport to bind the Director.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to maintain flexible, non-binding guidelines for declining IPR institution, reducing the risk that their patents will be subjected to additional administrative challenges.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies and other frequent IPR petitioners like Apple, Google, Cisco, and Intel lose their challenge to PTO discretionary denial practices, meaning the PTO can continue declining institution of IPR proceedings without formal rulemaking constraints.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Board correctly construed claims of a patent directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether the challenged claims would have been obvious over prior art.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Board's claim construction in an IPR proceeding involving DivX's patent on streaming partly encrypted media content. The Board's majority had adopted a disputed construction that enabled it to reject Netflix's obviousness challenge. The court vacated the Board's decision and remanded for further proceedings under the correct claim construction.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming services and technology companies benefit from the court's correction of claim construction errors that could otherwise shield patents from valid obviousness challenges in IPR proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the digital media and encryption space face continued uncertainty as remanded IPR proceedings may ultimately invalidate their claims under corrected claim constructions.

impacts.caseName: Game Plan, Inc. v. Uninterrupted IP, LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Trademark Trial and Appeal Board correctly cancelled a registered trademark and dismissed an opposition based on likelihood of confusion and priority of common law rights acquired through assignment.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the TTAB's cancellation of Game Plan's registration for a stylized 'I AM MORE THAN AN ATHLETE' mark and dismissal of its opposition to UNIP's six intent-to-use applications. UNIP acquired common law rights predating Game Plan's registration through a 2019 assignment that transferred both the mark and associated goodwill. The court upheld the Board's finding that UNIP had priority based on these assigned common law rights.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses can acquire and rely on common law trademark rights through valid assignments that include goodwill transfer to establish priority and cancel competing registrations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Trademark registrants face cancellation risk from parties who acquire earlier common law rights through assignment, even when the assignment occurs after opposition proceedings have commenced.

impacts.caseName: In re Bayou Grande Coffee Roasting Co.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the TTAB erred in applying the doctrine of foreign equivalents to refuse registration of the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops based on its meaning as Kashmiri green tea.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the TTAB's refusal to register the mark KAHWA for cafés and coffee shops. The Board had found the mark generic or merely descriptive based on its meaning as Kashmiri green tea under the doctrine of foreign equivalents. The court's reversal allows a coffee shop chain with fourteen locations to proceed with trademark registration.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses using foreign-language terms as brand names benefit from limits on the doctrine of foreign equivalents, particularly where the foreign term describes a product not commonly associated with the applicant's services in the U.S. market.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Office of Personnel Management's regulatory writing requirement for overtime authorization under 5 C.F.R. § 550.111(c) is a valid exercise of rulemaking authority under 5 U.S.C. § 5542(a), which requires payment for overtime 'officially ordered or approved.'
impacts.description: In an en banc decision, the Federal Circuit held that OPM's longstanding regulatory requirement that overtime be authorized in writing is a valid exercise of its delegated rulemaking authority. The case arose from a class action by IHS nurses who alleged they were denied overtime pay for pandemic-related work. The court affirmed dismissal of the overtime claim, finding the 80-year-old writing requirement is a permissible implementation of the statutory 'officially ordered or approved' standard.
impacts.positiveImplications: Federal agencies benefit from the validation of the writing requirement as a tool for managing and controlling overtime expenditures, providing clear administrative standards for overtime authorization.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal employees who perform uncompensated overtime work without written pre-authorization face significant barriers to recovering backpay, even when supervisors were aware of and benefited from the additional work.

impacts.caseName: Secretary of Defense v. Pratt & Whitney
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an agreement between the government and a defense contractor regarding inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool for cost-plus contracts was valid, and whether the Board's decision on the overhead allocation base was final and appealable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit addressed government cost-plus contract disputes with Pratt & Whitney regarding overhead cost allocation between government and commercial engine programs. The court dismissed the appeal on the overhead allocation base for lack of jurisdiction (not a final decision), but reversed the Board's finding that an enforceable agreement existed regarding inclusion of 'Drag' costs in the overhead pool, holding the agreement invalid.
impacts.positiveImplications: The government benefits from the invalidation of the Drag cost agreement, potentially reducing its share of overhead costs under cost-plus defense contracts with major contractors.
impacts.negativeImplications: Defense contractors using collaboration agreements with parts suppliers face scrutiny over how costs from those unconventional arrangements are allocated to government contracts under Cost Accounting Standards.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the International Trade Commission's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations is consistent with the governing statute and whether section 1516a abrogates the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential is incompatible with the governing trade statute and that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial records. The court treated the ITC's mandamus petition as an appeal under the collateral order doctrine and affirmed the Court of International Trade's order directing the ITC to comply with the statute's confidentiality requirements.
impacts.positiveImplications: Importers and trade litigants benefit from increased transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, as the ITC can no longer automatically shield all questionnaire responses from public access.
impacts.negativeImplications: Domestic producers and parties submitting commercially sensitive information in trade investigations face increased risk of public disclosure, potentially discouraging full cooperation with ITC questionnaires.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court correctly denied JMOL of obviousness and a new trial on damages for a patent covering pre-lit artificial trees with integrated mechanical and electrical trunk connections.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's denial of Polygroup's motion for JMOL of obviousness and new trial on damages regarding Willis's '186 patent for pre-lit artificial trees. The patent claimed a design where assembling modular trunk sections simultaneously establishes mechanical and electrical connections, eliminating manual wiring steps. The court upheld the jury's findings on non-obviousness and damages.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with consumer product innovations benefit from the court's affirmation that combining known elements to achieve a practical improvement in assembly can be non-obvious, supporting robust patent protection for product design innovations.
impacts.negativeImplications: Competitors in the consumer products space face continued exposure to patent infringement claims and damages for products incorporating features found to be non-obvious combinations of prior art elements.

impacts.caseName: Micron Technology Inc. v. Longhorn IP LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Idaho Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act is preempted by federal patent law, and whether the Federal Circuit has jurisdiction over an interlocutory bond order under the Act.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit dismissed for lack of jurisdiction an appeal from an $8 million bond imposed under Idaho's Bad Faith Assertions of Patent Infringement Act. Patent assertion entities Longhorn IP and Katana Silicon Technologies had sued Micron for infringement of expired semiconductor patents. The district court found a reasonable likelihood of bad faith assertion and imposed the bond, but the Federal Circuit determined it lacked appellate jurisdiction over the interlocutory order.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies targeted by patent assertion entities benefit from state anti-patent-troll laws that can impose bond requirements, as the Federal Circuit's dismissal leaves the bond in place pending final resolution.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent assertion entities face significant financial burdens from state bad-faith patent assertion statutes, and the inability to obtain immediate appellate review of bond orders increases litigation risk and costs.
